73580.subject:
The Unicorn That Started With a $100,000 Grant and No Plan | One Block Weekly
 #12
73580.body:
Hello,

Welcome back to One Block Weekly. One coin, one story.

Most financial exchanges took decades to build. This one was coded by a single engineer who had been laid off four months earlier.

🦄 Uniswap (UNI)

Hayden Adams lost his job at Siemens in 2017. A friend suggested he learn Ethereum smart contract development to keep busy.

He did. Then he built an exchange.

Uniswap launched in November 2018 with a radical idea: instead of matching buyers and sellers, anyone could deposit funds into a liquidity pool and let an algorithm set the price. No order books, no market makers, no company in the middle.

Vitalik Buterin had sketched the concept in a Reddit post. Adams built it into reality, funded by a $100,000 grant from the Ethereum Foundation.

What started as a solo project became the backbone of decentralised finance.

⚡ A Few Moments That Shaped Uniswap

2020 - DeFi summer arrived and Uniswap was at the centre of it. Trading volume exploded as new tokens launched daily and traders rushed to swap them before centralised exchanges had even listed them.

September 2020 - Uniswap airdropped 400 UNI tokens to every wallet that had ever used the protocol. At peak prices that was worth over $16,000. One of the most generous token distributions in crypto history.

2020 - SushiSwap copied the code and launched a vampire attack, draining over $1 billion in liquidity. Uniswap responded by launching its own token and reclaimed its position. The attack accelerated its growth.

2021 - Uniswap V3 launched with concentrated liquidity, letting providers allocate funds within specific price ranges for far greater capital efficiency.

🔢 What Makes It Different

Uniswap runs on smart contracts with no company controlling the trades. Anyone can list a token, provide liquidity, or trade without an account or identity check.

The UNI token gives holders governance rights over a treasury worth billions. It remains one of the largest and most active DAOs in existence.

🤯 Three Weird Uniswap Facts

One grant changed everything. Without the Ethereum Foundation's $100,000, Uniswap might not have survived its first year. That single investment helped build a protocol that eventually processed trillions in trading volume.

The SEC came after it too. In 2024 the SEC issued a Wells notice to Uniswap Labs. Uniswap pushed back aggressively, arguing the protocol is neutral technology, not a securities exchange. The battle is ongoing.

The unicorn was not accidental. Uniswap's mascot is a nod to its improbable origins. It became one of the most recognisable symbols in DeFi, built by someone who had never written a smart contract before starting.

Hayden Adams went from unemployed to founder of one of the most used financial protocols ever built, in about a year.

Crypto has produced wilder stories, but not many.

More coins, more stories, coming soon.

Explore UNI ( https://app.youhodler.com/wallets/uni?utm_medium=email )
Ex

73577.subject: How SCOTUS both checks and empowers Donald Trump
73577.body:
The Economist’s week ahead

Автор: The Economist


Автор: The Economist

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/how-scotus-both-checks-empowers-donald-trump-the-economist-chg9e?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BG%2BnV4UcoQcmz2wx50AYLoQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=1DroZ5jFm6CIg1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mpl4rjfs~1l-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mpl4rjfs-1l
  
Enjoying this newsletter? Get it in your inbox every weekend, as well as our daily round-up of The Economist’s best journalism, by signing up for free here. America’s Supreme Court still has some big cases to decide this term. It has held the line on Donald Trump’s most egregious grabs at powers the constitution clearly doesn’t give him. But on some concrete policy areas it has let him—and looks set to go on letting him—move the country sharply to the right. Colombia has three candidates running for president, any one of which has a shot. One is a populist right-winger in the style of El Salvador’s dictator Nayib Bukele; one is a leftist with a soft spot for making peace with former guerrillas; one is an establishment conservative. It is a hugely consequential vote, and if the first two reach the runoff, could be hugely polarising too. What to expect in the week ahead: ▸ Next week will see a host of central-bank meetings. Israel is expected to cut interest rates slightly on Monday; a buoyant shekel has kept war-fuelled inflation at bay. Hungary and Sri Lanka follow on Tuesday, New Zealand on Wednesday, and South Korea and South Africa on Thursday. ▸ Salesforce, a software giant, reports its first-quarter earnings on Wednesday. Investors are sceptical about whether it can survive the artificial-intelligence revolution; its shares are down 30% year-to-date. ▸ The foreign ministers of the Quad, an Indo-Pacific grouping of America, Australia, India and Japan, meet in New Delhi on Tuesday. Marco Rubio, America’s secretary of state, is set to attend. Asia’s energy crisis, triggered by the war in Iran, is likely to top the agenda. Lane Greene, Senior digital editor Editor’s picks How the Supreme Court both checks and empowers Donald Trump Donald Trump's most enduring legacy may be his imprint on the Supreme Court. In his first term he appointed three justices—Neil Gorsuch, Brett Kavanaugh and Amy Coney Barrett—creating a 6-3 conservative majority. The court has since delivered several landmark rulings applauded by the president and his supporters. The constitutional right to abortion is gone; affirmative action in university admissions has been curtailed; and former presidents now enjoy broad immunity for official acts. More recently the court has provisionally blessed Mr Trump’s policies on spending, immigration and the restruc

near
$77K
this morning. US markets are closed today for Memorial Day,
keeping volume light.

The week ended with more questions than answers about Bitcoin's
direction. But a few stories cut through the noise. A legal
settlement closed a chapter on FTX. A key regulatory provision
could reshape how crypto yield works entirely. And a security
crisis in one country is raising uncomfortable questions about
what KYC data collection actually costs ordinary holders.

Let's dive in.

Latest News
-----------

Bitcoin Just Ended Its Longest Underperformance Streak Ever

Bitcoin just ended a 142-day stretch of underperformance
against the S&P 500, the longest in its history. Former Credit
Suisse portfolio chief Mark Connors said BTC is now entering an
outperformance phase.

He described BTC as having a consistent pattern of absorbing
early macro losses before recovering ahead of other assets.

What macro conditions does Connors say make bonds vulnerable
right now?

73565.subject: The secret AI page Apple won't show you
73565.body:
----------
######                      [Sign Up](http://utm.genai.works/r/gg2ubr5) | [Collaborate with us](https://6hhfxazcbpc.typeform.com/to/uXIl0IFH?utm_source=atlas&utm_medium=newsletter&utm_campaign=general_2026_05_11&utm_content=banner_post)


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/8776fa6e-e2d0-4aec-9648-1bed6de3a659/GenAI_Daily_Banner.jpg?t=1778687445)
Caption: 


----------

----------
**Welcome back!** Apple registered [genai.apple.com](https://genai.apple.com) and left it blank. That empty page is its own kind of announcement with WWDC two weeks away. The model behind Grok tripled in size. Goldman's CEO took to the New York Times to calm the AI job fears his own economists helped start. And McKinsey is rethinking how it bills now that AI does in an afternoon what makes them consultants.

———————————————————————————

**In today's Generative AI Newsletter:**

* **Apple:** What is Apple cooking at genai.apple.com, and why is the page still blank?

* **Grok:** The model behind Grok just tripled in size. What does that get you?

* **Goldman Sachs:** Why is the CEO downplaying the job loss his own economists measured?

* **McKinsey:** What happens to million-dollar advice when AI does it in an afternoon?


----------

----------
## [GenAI ](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/05/23/apple-gen-ai-subdomain/)~~[Works](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/05/23/apple-gen-ai-subdomain/)~~[…Apple?](https://www.macrumors.com/2026/05/23/apple-gen-ai-subdomain/)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/45c8768c-55f9-4ffd-8a7e-61d06b82a189/Beehiiv_03_-_May_25.png?t=1779699986)
Caption: 

Apple registered genai.apple.com and the page does not load yet. WWDC starts June 8 and Apple promised AI news across **iOS 27, iPadOS 27 and macOS 27**.

The top selling phone maker already has an Apple Intelligence page. This leaves the possibilities open, but people are expecting one of two things. 

Either Apple thinks everyone mistrusts Apple Intelligence name so badly it needs rebranding. 

Or Apple is keeping them both but each has different features.

The expected star is a rebuilt Siri you can actually go back and forth with. On-screen awareness is also on the list plus live captions on video and it can read nutrition labels and business cards.

Apple has spent two years talking about AI more than shipping it. We’re excited to see what they’ve got. 


----------

----------
## [New partnership between GenAI Works and Oracle. The company is now raising its next round.](https://utm.genai.works/r/484ur9w)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/3a7f7b84-38cf-47d7-bb13-6d19afcb1c37/image.png?t=1779701391)
Follow image link: (https://utm.genai
